lesson: Caching zobristKey results in a Map eliminates redundant fnv1a64 string hashing. High cache hit rate because most game state is unchanged between moves.

Trace/debug infrastructure (effectPath strings, withTracePath context spreads, resolveTraceProvenance objects, emitTrace argument construction) is a significant hidden cost when called 100K+ times. Skip ALL trace-related work when tracing is disabled — check the trace-enabled flag BEFORE doing any string construction, object creation, or context spreading.

V8 JIT optimizes object spreads, regex, and BigInt XOR efficiently. Micro-optimizations that try to avoid these patterns (Object.create(null), manual string parsing, split bigint pairs) are consistently SLOWER due to overhead exceeding savings or breaking V8 hidden classes.

Deep profiling before guessing prevents wasted experiments. The assumed bottleneck (setVar state spreads at 127ms) was dwarfed by the actual bottleneck (phase lifecycle effects at 17000ms, agent double-validation at 10000ms, listPlayers array allocation at 372K calls). ALWAYS profile first — add opt-in instrumentation to any depth.

Agent preparePlayableMoves was calling legalChoicesEvaluate + evaluatePlayableMoveCandidate (double-validation) for every legal move. Replacing with single-pass probeMoveViability eliminated 53% of agent time. Redundant validation across call boundaries is the #1 optimization target.

resolvePlayerSel called listPlayers (Array.from allocation) on EVERY call including the fast-path 'chosen' selector. With 372K pvar lookups, this created 372K unnecessary arrays. Fast-pathing the chosen selector with a bounds check instead saved 3.6%. Look for hidden allocations inside high-frequency selector/resolver functions.

V8 hidden class monomorphism is critical. Fast-paths that create objects with different property shapes at the same call site cause catastrophic deoptimization (17% regression in exp-026). Even different return points in the SAME function with the SAME property keys create polymorphism. Only optimize within the existing object creation pattern.

Functions called on every move (like dispatchTriggers) that are no-ops for certain game configurations (0 triggers) should fast-path before doing any work. The array copy [...triggerLog], resource creation, and graph resolution in dispatchTriggers were ~630ms of wasted work across 50 games.

advanceToDecisionPoint called legalMoves() just to check .length > 0, enumerating ALL legal moves across all actions. Adding earlyExitAfterFirst option stops after finding the first legal move, saving 3.39%. Pattern: any existence check that calls an enumerate-all function should use an early-exit variant.

computeAlwaysCompleteActionIds excluded parameterized actions, forcing probeMoveViability probes for every raise legal move (~10K probes/game). Actions with params ARE always-complete if their effect tree has no decision effects (chooseOne/chooseN/rollRandom). Removing the params.length > 0 exclusion saved 10.61%. LESSON: overly conservative exclusion logic can create massive hidden costs in hot paths.

Passing a profiler object through the kernel hot path caused 34% overhead — NOT from the profiling calls themselves but from V8 JIT deoptimization. The profiler field on EffectContext created polymorphic object shapes that prevented V8 from inlining and optimizing hot path functions. Stripping the profiler from kernel options in the simulator (keeping only sim-level timing) saved 23.94%. CRITICAL LESSON: never thread optional diagnostic objects through hot-path function signatures. Keep instrumentation at the boundary, not throughout the call graph.

buildIntRangeCandidates built full 960-element arrays for raise amount ranges that were immediately downsampled to 10. Direct mathematical computation of the downsampled subset (O(maxResults * required) vs O(range)) eliminated 41.8% of legalMoves time. Pattern: when selecting a small subset from a large range, compute the subset directly instead of materializing the full range.

evalValue concat handler used .map() + .filter() + .map().join() = 3 intermediate arrays per call. With 100K+ concat evals, this was 300K+ unnecessary array allocations (41% of CPU). Replacing with single-pass for loop + string concatenation saved 27.95%. ALSO: aggregate handler used .map() with per-item { ...ctx, bindings: { ...ctx.bindings, [bind]: item } } = 2 spreads per item. Replacing with mutable bindings object (safe for synchronous evalValue) eliminated all per-item spreads. CRITICAL LESSON: .map().filter().reduce() chains in hot paths are allocation bombs. Always use for loops in performance-critical code.

resolveBindingTemplate ran a regex /\{([^{}]+)\}/g on EVERY call. In Texas Hold'em, 100% of templates are plain strings with no {binding} placeholders. A single indexOf('{') === -1 check eliminated 20.54% of total runtime. CRITICAL LESSON: regex-based string processing in hot paths MUST have a fast-path for the common case (no matches). This will also benefit FITL if many of its template strings are plain.

Inlining logic into evalValue (exp-014: switch dispatch + inline validation) caused V8 JIT deoptimization — the function became too large for inlining. Lazy closures (exp-015) were MORE expensive than objects. V8 is extremely sensitive to function size in the 100K+ call-per-game range. LESSON: for ultra-hot functions like evalValue (~2M calls/game), do NOT make the function body larger. Keep it small and delegate to helper functions that V8 can inline individually.

After interpreter optimizations (mutable context, concat/aggregate loops, binding template short-circuit, profiler removal), the compiled lifecycle effect path became 12-16% SLOWER than the interpreter. The compiled path's per-fragment overhead (normalizeFragmentResult, context spreading) exceeds the interpreter's optimized per-effect overhead (mutable workCtx, direct dispatch). LESSON: compiled effect paths need to be re-benchmarked after interpreter optimizations — they may no longer provide a net benefit.

In resolveRef, 'binding' refs are the most common type in effect chains but were handled last in a 14-way if-else chain. Moving the binding check to the first position eliminated ~700K unnecessary string comparisons per game, saving 3.51%. Pattern: profile which discriminant is most frequent in hot-path dispatch chains and put it first.

Nested effect scopes (if/let/forEach calling applyEffectsWithBudgetState) re-created mutable state clones + DraftTracker Sets even though the parent scope already had a mutable state. Checking cursor.tracker to skip redundant createMutableState/createDraftTracker saved 8%. Pattern: any scope-boundary setup that is idempotent should check if the parent already did it.

createSeatResolutionContext rebuilds 5 Maps + arrays from scratch on every call. For FITL with 30K+ probeMoveViability calls per benchmark, this was the #1 redundant computation. A WeakMap cache keyed on def.seats (stable reference) with playerCount validation eliminated 6.31% of runtime. Pattern: any function that builds Maps/Sets from GameDef fields and is called per-move or per-probe should use WeakMap caching.

Adding ANY new field to EffectCursor or GameDefRuntime interfaces causes V8 hidden class deoptimization (4-7% regression). Even optional fields that are always present within a scope break monomorphism. This was proven across 5 experiments (exp-003: pipelineActionIds on GameDefRuntime, exp-006: triggerIndex on GameDefRuntime, exp-013: arena on EffectCursor). CRITICAL: never add properties to hot-path object interfaces for optimization purposes.

Import aliasing (import { X as Y }) in hot-path modules causes V8 JIT deoptimization (6.82% regression). V8 inlines based on exact function identity; aliased imports change the identity at the call site, breaking monomorphic dispatch. Never rename exports via aliased imports in performance-critical modules.

Branch reordering for early-exit optimization (moving phase check before contract evaluation) caused 4% regression in FITL. V8 JIT optimizes branch prediction based on the ORIGINAL ordering profile. Also, for...in iteration is consistently slower than Set-based iteration in V8 (4.2% regression). V8 prefers typed collection iteration over property enumeration.

advanceToDecisionPoint calls legalMoves(earlyExitAfterFirst) in a loop. Checking trivial actions (no params, no pre, always-complete) FIRST when earlyExitAfterFirst eliminates O(actions) per iteration. For FITL: 41% improvement.

V8 hidden class deoptimization extends to ALL frequently-created objects in the kernel hot path (MoveEnumerationState, EffectCursor, GameDefRuntime, ReadContext). Adding ANY field causes 2-7% regression. Also: WeakMap/Map caching, frozen array iteration, and closure variable capture all cause V8 deopt in this codebase.

96% of FITL legalMoves cost is ENUMERATION (action preflights + pipeline predicate evaluation + decision sequence admission), only 4% is classification probes. Sim-level profiling attributed this to legalMoves total, hiding that the bottleneck is inside the enumeration effect execution calls, not the classification step.

Probes (probeMoveViability) are CHEAPER than agent completions (evaluatePlayableMoveCandidate). Probe stops at first decision (~1ms), completion resolves ALL decisions (~5-10ms). Never bypass probes to shift work to agents — 5-10x cost increase per move.

resolvePlayerSel called listPlayers + sortAndDedupePlayers for 'actor' and 'active' selectors that always resolve to a single known player ID. Fast-pathing to return [playerId] directly avoids listPlayers array allocation, Set creation, array spread, and sort — 4 allocations per call. With 15K+ calls per benchmark, saved 1.92%. Pattern: any selector resolving to a single known value should skip collection infrastructure.

Early exits in CALLEE functions work for hot-path optimization, even when modifying the CALLER's control flow fails. evaluateActionSelectorContracts had an early-exit check before Set creation that saved 1.11%. The key insight: V8 JIT optimizes the caller based on its branch structure. Changes inside callees don't disrupt the caller's JIT optimization. Modifying hot-path caller control flow causes V8 deopt, but modifying callee return paths does not.

WeakSet.has() as an 'already validated' cache is profitable when the validation cost exceeds the hash lookup cost AND the validation callee is in the PREFLIGHT path (not the kernel execution path). assertEvalRuntimeResourcesContract validated the same object 25 times per legalMoves call. A single WeakSet.has() check saved 1.48%. Contrasts with exp-012 where WeakSet on GameState inside legalChoicesDiscover caused +12.9% regression. Pattern: use WeakSet for idempotent validation caching in PREFLIGHT callees only.

Agent behavior changes (completionsPerTemplate, decision parameters) are NOT safe performance optimizations. Different move selections produce different game state trajectories with different legalMoves complexity. In FITL, reducing completions from 3 to 2 made the agent 27% faster but caused 16% slower legalMoves due to harder game states. Also: adding cross-module imports between agents/ and kernel/ hot-path modules causes catastrophic V8 deoptimization (13% regression from a single import).

Kernel computation function modifications are UNIFORMLY unsafe: direct construction replacing spread (exp-010: +8%), spread removal (exp-011: +11%), early exits in computation callees (exp-002: +2%, exp-007: +6%). V8 JIT has deeply profiled the kernel's object construction and iteration patterns. The ONLY safe modifications are to VALIDATION callees that perform no actual computation. ANY change to computation callees or construction patterns triggers hidden class polymorphism and JIT deopt.

resolveActionActorCore was creating eval context + calling resolvePlayerSel for every action preflight, but when action.actor === active (100% of FITL actions), the result is always applicable since decisionPlayer === activePlayer. A simple string equality check fast-path eliminated ~9000 unnecessary object allocations per game. Pattern: validation callees that check trivially-true properties of common selector values should short-circuit before any object creation.

Adding early returns to SMALL utility functions (like isWithinActionLimits, ~10 lines) that are called from hot-path callees causes catastrophic V8 deoptimization (+19% regression). V8 inlines small functions into their callers. Changing the inlined body changes the caller optimization profile. ONLY modify callees that are too large for V8 inlining (~60+ lines, like resolveActionActorCore at 30+ lines with try/catch). Small utility functions are effectively part of the caller at JIT time.

findAction in legal-choices.ts did def.actions.find() — O(n) linear scan through all actions — called 25+ times per legalMoves invocation via decision sequence discovery. A WeakMap-cached Map<ActionId, ActionDef> keyed on def.actions array (stable reference) saved 1.98%. Pattern: look for Array.find() and Array.some() calls on GameDef arrays (def.actions, def.zones, etc.) that execute inside the enumeration loop. These are hidden O(n) costs that multiply with call frequency.

Boosting VC's economic engine actions (Rally=3, Tax=2) over combat actions (weight 1) improved compositeScore by 16.2%. The VC victory formula (Opposition + Bases > 35) rewards building infrastructure over fighting. Simple action weight differentiation is high-leverage.

Weight-only changes (Tier 1) hit a ceiling when action-type ranking is already established. Within the same action type, candidates may score identically if no candidate-level features differentiate them. Candidate-level differentiation comes from working preview (projectedSelfMargin), completionScoreTerms, and candidateFeatures. Preview was fixed via specs 96-97 (decision-point state snapshots) and now provides real candidate-level differentiation — projectedMarginWeight=5 is the strongest scoring signal.

dropPassWhenOtherMovesExist pruning rule is ESSENTIAL for all profiles. Without it, pass scores 0 and ties with unscored actions; stableMoveKey picks pass alphabetically. When all players pass, the game never terminates (infinite loop). NEVER remove this rule.

Action weight values are a threshold effect: as long as the RANKING is Rally > Tax > Event > unscored, the exact numeric values don't matter. Rally=2 and Rally=5 produce identical outcomes. This is because most decision points have few candidates and the action-type ranking determines the choice, not the magnitude.

Preview system was fixed via specs 96-97 (decision-point state snapshots, global state aggregation). Previous attempts to bypass preview at the template level failed because moves lacked inner decisions. The architectural fix (state snapshots at decision points) resolved the root cause. Preview now evaluates candidates successfully (evaluatedCandidateCount > 0, unknownRefs: []) and projectedSelfMargin provides real candidate-level differentiation across action types.

candidate.paramCount (number of resolved decisions in a move) is NOT a good proxy for move quality. More params means more target zones for Rally, but spreading guerrillas across many zones is often worse than concentrating in strategic locations. Tiebreaker changes cascade through entire game trajectories across all seeds.

VC macro-strategy Rally>Tax>Event is near-optimal and MUST NOT be inverted. Tax-first causes catastrophic failure (games hitting maxTurns, margin -12). The VC economic engine is Rally-driven: guerrillas -> bases -> opposition. Tax is fuel for Rally, not a standalone strategy. Further improvement requires micro-level candidate differentiation, not macro-level ranking changes.

candidateParam for pipeline choice bindings ($noBaseChoice, $withBaseChoice, $targetSpaces) requires suffix/index matching in resolveCandidateParam. Direct lookup fails because move.params keys include full decision paths. The fix enables future experiments but candidate-level features may not TRIGGER if game conditions for certain options don't arise in the evaluation seed set.

completionScoreTerms with zoneProp enable candidate-level differentiation that breaks the action-type-weight ceiling. zoneProp returns 'unknown' type at compile time — must wrap in coalesce with a numeric fallback (e.g., coalesce: [zoneProp: {zone: {ref: option.value}, prop: population}, 0]) to satisfy the score term number type requirement. This pattern works for any zone attribute (population, econ, etc).

candidateParam ref syntax is `candidate.param.X` (not `candidateParam.X`). candidateFeatures using candidate.param.X fire for ALL move candidates that have parameter X, not just a specific action type. Must use `when: eq(feature.isActionName, true)` to restrict. Also: population-based scoring of agitate is WRONG — high-pop zones cost more to agitate in FITL, making them suboptimal targets.

Pipeline decision-sequence satisfiability probes (isMoveDecisionSequenceAdmittedForLegalMove) are redundant for template moves when the compiled first-decision domain check already validates the first decision. The probe executes the full pipeline effect tree (applyChooseN, evalQuery, filterTokensByExpr) just to verify satisfiability — work the agent handles gracefully at completion time via templateCompletionUnsatisfiable. Removing the probe saved 4.7% of total runtime. Pattern: defer validation to the consumer when the consumer has robust error handling.

FITL kernel computation functions (resolveRef, evalCondition, foldTokenFilterExpr, matchesScalarMembership) are at a V8 JIT optimization ceiling. ANY modification — WeakMap caching (+7.7%), fast-path branching (+3.8%), short-circuit evaluation (test failures) — causes hidden class deoptimization. The ONLY safe optimization pattern is removing WORK at the orchestration level (skipping calls entirely), not modifying how calls behave.

After exhausting local optimizations, the remaining FITL performance bottleneck is architectural: (1) object spread immutability costs 15% CPU — addressable via Foundation 11's scoped-draft-state carve-out, (2) interpretive AST evaluation costs 19% — addressable via AOT expression compilation, (3) string-based ID comparisons cost 9% — addressable via integer-interned domain IDs. These require spec-level architectural changes, not campaign-level experiments.

projectedMarginWeight=5 with working preview is the strongest scoring signal for VC agent. Reducing it from 5 to 1 caused -42.6% compositeScore regression (3.1333 -> 1.8). The preview system evaluates all non-pass candidates (evaluatedCandidateCount matches legal moves) and provides different projected margins per action type (e.g., terror=-30, attack=-40, event varies -60 to -30). Do NOT reduce projectedMarginWeight — it IS the primary decision driver.

Event preview DOES differentiate shaded vs unshaded card sides after Spec 109 implementation. Card-68 shaded scored -30 (margin -6) while unshaded scored -40 to -60 (margin -8 to -12). Preview correctly evaluates event quality. The prior belief that events were blind was based on pre-Spec-109 traces.

With working preview (projectedMarginWeight=5), all action-type weight considerations (preferEvent, preferTaxWeighted, preserveResources) are dead weight — preview correctly ranks actions by projected margin. Only preferRallyWeighted is essential (captures multi-turn infrastructure value preview can't see). The minimal VC profile is: preferProjectedSelfMargin + preferRallyWeighted + penalizeAttack + preferPopulousTargets + dropPassWhenOtherMovesExist + tiebreakers.

Tiny targeted PENALTIES (-0.1) work where bonuses fail. penalizeAttack(-0.1) flipped 2 margin-0 seeds to wins by redirecting tied attack→terror decisions. Key: penalty only affects ties (negligible vs 5-point preview weight), and attack-terror ties exist on losing seeds but not winning seeds (asymmetric effect). Action-type BONUSES (even 0.5) cascade catastrophically via butterfly effect on winning seeds.

Card annotations (activeCard.annotation.<side>.<metric>) are compiled for all 130 FITL event cards but annotation-based bonuses (w=2-3) cannot overcome the 20-point preview gap for capability/operation-granting events. At margin -8: terror projects -4 (score -20), capability event projects -8 (score -40). Bonus of +2-3 barely dents this. Multi-step preview (Spec 111) is needed to correctly value events that grant free operations.

DSL type nuance: activeCard.hasTag.* compiles to number (0/1), not boolean. activeCard.annotation.* compiles to number. globalMarker.* compiles to 'id' (string). Use type: number for tag/annotation features, type: id for marker features, gt: [ref, 0] for when clauses, coalesce with 0 (not false). boolToNumber requires boolean input — don't wrap tag refs in it.

Signal normalization is ESSENTIAL for multi-signal agents. Raw preview margin (range ±50) dominates any additive bonus (range 0-5). Use candidateAggregates with min/max to compute the margin range, then div in the consideration's value expression to normalize to [0,1]. With normalized margin at weight 5 (range 0-5), secondary signals (capabilities w=3, rally w=3) compete fairly. Normalization enabled +2 wins in FITL campaign (compositeScore 2.4667→3.6). IMPORTANT: normalize in considerations, not candidateFeatures (candidateFeatures cannot reference aggregates).

Duplicate candidates with identical stableMoveKey exist in FITL candidate lists (31 candidates, 15 unique). Duplicates arise from template completion exploring multiple paths to the same resolved move. No dedup step exists in preparePlayableMoves. Duplicates get preview: undefined (preview cache serves first occurrence only), distort normalized scoring aggregates, and waste computation. Spec 114 filed.

Surface ref type bug: compile-agents.ts:1813 hardcoded ALL surface refs as type 'number', but globalMarker.* returns string (marker state) and activeCard.id returns string (card identity). Fixed to return 'id' for globalMarker and activeCardIdentity families, 'unknown' for activeCardMetadata. Without this fix, eq(globalMarker.X, 'shaded') fails type checking.

Rally dominance is the winning VC strategy. Any non-Rally action preference (Terror, Events, Tax) causes regression across seeds. The Rally-first strategy builds guerrilla/base infrastructure that enables effective Coup agitation — the primary margin-gaining mechanism.

Normalized margin scoring outperforms raw margin at scale. Raw margin worked better at 3 seeds but caused regression at 10+ seeds because Rally's bonus (weight 5) couldn't compete with raw margin differences (range -40 to +10). Normalized margin compresses the range to [0,1], letting secondary signals (Rally, capability) compete fairly.

Features that test as dead weight at low seed counts may become essential at higher seed counts. valueCapabilityGain and preferPopulousTargets were removed as simplifications at tier 3 (3 seeds) but re-adding them at tier 15 each flipped an additional seed from loss/draw to win.

Opponent margin awareness via preview.victory.currentMargin.<seatName> is supported but cannot prevent structurally unwinnable seeds. US jumps from margin -13 to +1 entirely through Coup pacification (multi-step post-decision process). No pre-Coup VC action can prevent this swing. Defensive play slightly degrades winning seed margins without flipping any losses.

Enriched tournament traces (all-seat margins, opponent moves, per-seat action summaries) are essential for data-driven agent evolution. Without them, the campaign spent ~20 experiments guessing at opponent behavior. With them, structural unwinnability was proven in 1 experiment.

completionPolicy: required on free operation grants can deadlock the game when no legal completion exists (template completion fails). FITL Card 75 (Sihanoukville shaded, march from rally zones) triggers this on seed 1009. Needs skipIfNoLegalCompletion policy. Filed as FREOPSKIP-001.

Double function calls in ternary conditions (e.g., f().length > 0 ? f() : default) and double-call patterns (f() === undefined ? {} : { key: f() }) are low-hanging fruit in orchestration loops. Caching in a local variable is zero-risk and can yield 6%+ when the functions have non-trivial cost. Also, replacing .some() predicates with Set.has() for membership checks on stable collections eliminates O(n) per call.

Returning a singleton vs freshly-constructed object from the same function causes V8 JIT deoptimization (+6.5%). V8 inline caches at call sites assume consistent object identity patterns — polymorphic returns (sometimes pre-existing reference, sometimes new) poison the cache even when shapes match. Creating new Map() inside a function that V8 has profiled also causes deopt (+3.6%). The ONLY safe allocation reduction in this codebase is eliminating calls entirely (exp-001/002 pattern), not changing how existing calls return values.

Completion-scope considerations (scopes: [completion]) change which COMPLETED moves are generated, which then get different move-scope scores. Adding a completion-scope consideration can change which action TYPE is selected — not just which parameters. This is the completion→move entanglement documented in Spec 121.

Without explicit preview mode, the compiler defaults to exactWorld preview (compile-agents.ts:669). projectedSelfMargin DOES vary across candidates by default — preview is always active. The misconception that 'without preview all candidates get the same margin' wastes experiments. Check traces before assuming preview behavior.

Tests that use PolicyAgent-driven gameplay to reach specific game states are fragile under profile evolution. They break when ANY profile changes because different agent choices produce different game states. Use serialized state fixtures (deserializeGameState) to decouple test state setup from profile evolution. Foundation 2 violation.

Completion-scope considerations (e.g. preferPopulousTargets) have immediate measurable impact on margin. Profiles that lack completion-scope scoring leave target selection unguided. Adding population-weighted target selection is a quick win for victory-formula optimization.

Conditional strategy considerations (when clauses) can fundamentally alter game trajectories. trainWhenFewTroops prevented early opponent victory by building COIN control infrastructure. Small conditional weights (4) can override action preferences when conditions are met, creating dramatically different game outcomes.

The self keyword in globalTokenAgg tokenFilter does NOT match uppercase faction names (e.g., ARVN). Use literal faction strings instead. Discovered when arvnTroopCount always read 0 — the case mismatch between seat ID (lowercase) and token faction prop (uppercase) is a DSL quirk.

Coup redeployment phases can generate 75+ individual moves that consume the entire maxTurns budget. Batch redeployment (removing targetSpace param, using per-troop chooseOne completion) reduces template explosion but requires fully parameterless actions to also reduce move count. enumerateLegalMoves cannot probe parameterless actions with forEach.over mapSpaces in effects — see Spec 123.

Replacing Govern with Patrol as primary ARVN action causes severe regression (-4 to -24). Patrol does not help early game when ARVN lacks troop positioning. Govern (Patronage) is far more impactful than Patrol (COIN control) for ARVN margin.

The self/active keywords in agent policy globalTokenAgg token filters resolved to numeric PlayerId, not seat ID strings. Token props store faction as uppercase strings (e.g. ARVN). The strict equality comparison always failed, making token count features permanently 0. Fix: resolve self/active to seat ID string and use case-insensitive string comparison.

Phase 1 scoring ORIGINALLY skipped costClass=preview features for template operations, making projectedSelfMargin identical for all operations. Spec 63 (IMPLEMENTED AND MERGED) added Phase 1 representative preview: one template completion per action type enables preview-based discrimination. Opt-in via profile config: preview.phase1: true. Without this flag, the old behavior persists (action-type weights are the only Phase 1 differentiator). Profiles MUST enable phase1: true to benefit from preview at Phase 1.

Removing noisy action-type considerations (preferEvent, preserveResources, preferPatrolAction, preferSweepWeighted, preferAssaultWeighted, trainWhenFewTroops) and relying on preview-driven scoring with Phase 1 preview FLIPPED a seed from loss to win. The noisy considerations added fixed bonuses that overwhelmed preview margin differences between action types. Minimal profiles (projectedSelfMargin + 1-2 key action weights + completion-scope scoring) outperform complex profiles. This pattern held for both VC (Rally-first) and ARVN (Govern-first).

Normalized margin scoring (preferNormalizedMargin) REGRESSES ARVN performance — destroys seed 1000 win. ARVN needs RAW margin x weight, not normalized [0,1] range. The absolute margin differences between action types are the crucial signal. This is the OPPOSITE of what worked for VC. VC benefited from normalization because Rally bonus needed to compete with margin; ARVN benefits from raw magnitude because preview margin IS the primary decision driver. Normalization benefit is faction/game-dependent, not universal.

Single-step preview cannot differentiate opponent impact for ARVN actions. Govern/Train/Sweep do not change opponent victory margin in one step. globalTokenAgg counts (VC guerrillas, bases) are also uniform because ARVN operations activate guerrillas (state change) but do not remove them (count change). Only Assault removes tokens, but the Phase 1 random representative may target a zone with no activated guerrillas, producing no differentiation. Spec 124 (best-of-N representative selection) addresses this.

In COIN agent evolution, raw projectedSelfMargin at high weight creates identical traces for all secondary signal changes — the margin deltas between candidates exceed any practical secondary signal magnitude. Solution: combine raw margin at REDUCED weight (3) with a strong normalized margin consideration (weight 8), allowing secondary signals (action-type bonuses) to compete when margin differences are small.

In COIN games with independent victory tracks, opponent margin tracking (defensive considerations) produces constant offsets that dont change the argmax. ARVN actions dont meaningfully affect VC projected margin because their victory formulas operate on different state variables. Defensive signals only work when the agents actions directly impact the opponents victory metric.

Coup sub-phase decisions (redeployment, pacification) can produce 58+ perfectly tied candidates per game. Without completion-scoped considerations targeting these action types, troop positioning is resolved alphabetically by zone name. Always check per-decision gap distributions during OBSERVE — strategic decisions (ops/events) have large gaps while tactical decisions (coup phases) may have zero gaps.

The coalesce operator eagerly evaluates ALL branches via evaluateExprList. When a preview.var.* surface ref is the first coalesce branch and returns unknown, it unconditionally stamps candidate.previewOutcome, contaminating the outcome even when the coalesce successfully falls through to the non-preview branch. Fix: dont stamp previewOutcome on individual ref failure — let syncPreviewMetadata determine the canonical outcome from the preview surface.

V8 JIT deoptimization from polymorphic return shapes extends beyond kernel to agent module. Adding an early-return function (checkProfileResolutionFast) that returns objects with different property sets than the existing failureWithMetadata path caused +4% regression even though it eliminated canonicalization work. The V8 deopt cost exceeds the saved computation. Pattern: all return sites in a hot function must return objects with identical property sets.

Compiled closures (JIT-optimized function bodies) must not have their internal structure changed. Inlining a matchesResolvedPredicate call into direct === comparison in a compiled token filter closure caused +1.93% regression. V8 JIT optimizes closure bodies as units — structural changes (even simplifications) reset the JIT profile. This extends the small-utility-function lesson to ANY closure called 10K+ times per game.

After 5 prior perf campaigns + this campaign (7 experiments, 1 accept, 6 rejects), the FITL engine has reached V8 JIT optimization ceiling for micro-optimizations. The only safe pattern is removing entire function calls at the orchestration level. Remaining bottlenecks are architectural: object spread immutability (12% CPU, addressable via Spec 128 full-scope draft state), interpretive AST evaluation (8% CPU, partially addressed by Spec 120), string-based IDs (6% CPU, addressable via Spec 129 integer interning), conditional spreads creating megamorphic loads (6% CPU, addressable via Spec 130 canonical shapes).

Completion-scoped considerations (scopes: [completion]) with option.value refs still work post-Spec-140 and are the correct tool for per-option preferences at chooseOne microturns. The cookbook flags them as 'retired' for new authoring, but the engine actively evaluates them via selectBestCompletionChooseOneValue / buildCompletionChooseCallback. When an action has multiple options tied at score 0 in move-scope considerations, stableMoveKey breaks the tie alphabetically and often picks a suboptimal value. Add a completion-scoped consideration with option.value equality + weight to flip the choice reliably. Worked example: ARVN govern mode chooseOne over [aid, patronage] always picked 'aid' (alphabetical); adding preferPatronageMode (weight 10) gained +5.5 compositeScore by flipping all 54 govern-mode picks to 'patronage'.

Faction-agent evolution campaigns hit an architectural ceiling under the post-Spec-140 microturn kernel: every action-selection candidate's preview returns 'unresolved' / 'notDecisionComplete', so preferProjectedSelfMargin (the largest scoring weight in every shipped FITL profile) collapses to a constant. Without per-candidate margin signal, only action-type ranking + completion-scope option-equality tricks work; any experiment that tries to trade against the dominant action type regresses because the agent has no nuanced state-projection signal. Verified in code: policy-preview.ts:172-180 rejects pendingAdmissible candidates as notDecisionComplete; tryApplyPreview calls applyMove with advanceToDecisionPoint:false so even non-rejected candidates land at the first inner microturn with no params bound. This blocks meaningful Tier-1 progress past 'best action-type ranking found.' Resolution: Spec 145 (bounded synthetic-completion preview).

Adding a move-scope consideration to push a non-dominant action type is a no-op when the dominant action type is consistently available. Boosted preferEvent (eventWeight=5) for ARVN evolved profile produced ZERO behavior change vs baseline because Govern (+10) always beat events (+5) in every observed action-selection. To make a non-dominant action competitive you must either raise its weight ABOVE the dominant action's weight (which over-promotes it everywhere) OR add a conditional that fires only in states where the dominant action is unavailable. Generic boosts of underused considerations are not a productive experiment direction.

Any experiment that displaces Govern-patronage in ARVN-evolved profile regresses, even when the new action has a plausible game-theoretic justification. exp-002 (sweepWhenVcStrong w=12), exp-004 (trainWhenPatronageHigh w=10), exp-005 (preferHighPopulationTarget completion-scope) all looked sensible and all regressed. Root cause: under broken preview, ARVN cannot evaluate the actual margin trade-off between candidates, so any reduction in Govern count is a ~1-3 point margin loss per displaced govern (population in patronage spaces) for an unmeasured indirect benefit (VC disruption / COIN control build / future Coup pacification). Direct Govern margin gain dominates indirect setup until preview lets the agent see the trade.

After Spec 145, expect previewOutcome: 'ready' rate above ~80% on full FITL games for non-stochastic action-selection candidates. Rates well below this in new campaigns suggest completionDepthCap is too tight, preview.topK is too tight, or preview.mode is disabled; inspect previewDriveDepth, previewCompletionPolicy, and previewGatedCount before re-baselining.

When a kernel module already exposes a WeakMap-cached factory next to a raw builder (e.g., createSeatResolutionContext beside buildSeatResolutionIndex), agent-side code paths that construct fresh ephemeral runtimes (PolicyEvaluationContext, PolicyPreviewRuntime) often bypass the cache and call the raw builder directly. Audit `grep -n buildSeatResolutionIndex packages/engine/src/agents/` (and similar) and switch to the cached factory — works in ANY hot path that re-constructs evaluation contexts per option/candidate. -3.45% in this campaign on top of the prior +6.31% in fitl-perf-optimization.

Dead-code completion paths: when a game's profiles have no `scopes: [completion]` considerations, the agentGuided picker's selectBestCompletionChooseOneValue / buildCompletionChooseCallback always short-circuit with empty considerationIds, but callers still pay request-object allocation + the function-call overhead per inner-microturn pick. Detect at runtime construction (createPolicyRuntimeProviders) by scanning profile.use.considerations for any completion scope; if none, omit agentGuidedDeps entirely so pickAgentGuidedChooseOneDecision early-returns at the `guided === undefined` check. -1.89% on FITL preview perf. WeakMap-cache the scan keyed on the CompiledAgentProfile (stable reference).

publishMicroturn(def, state, runtime) is FAR more expensive than expected — ~80ms each in this codebase. The synthetic-completion driver called it once at the start of every drive but `input.state` is invariant across all candidates running through one createPolicyPreviewRuntime instance. A single closure variable `cachedOrigin` (lazy-init) eliminated ~150 redundant calls and saved -18.97% on FITL preview perf. Pattern: any per-iteration kernel call that takes only the runtime's invariant inputs can be lazy-cached at the runtime construction level. Closure vars are V8-safe; do NOT add a new field to the input/output shape.

Per-stateHash caching of derivePlayerObservation results within a preview runtime saves -1.94% in FITL preview perf. The function iterates ALL zones (66 in FITL) and allocates 3 maps + per-zone arrays even though only `hiddenSamplingZones` is consumed by the preview path. With ~200 ready/stochastic outcomes per benchmark and partial state-hash convergence across candidate drives (capability-card collapses, op-grant variants), the cache hit rate is enough for measurable savings. Pattern: any stateHash-dependent kernel call whose result is used downstream by a runtime-scoped consumer is a candidate for `Map<bigint, T>` caching keyed on `state.stateHash`.

Spread-based mutations of GameState ({...state, decisionStack: newStack, ...}) carry over BOTH stateHash and _runningHash unchanged from the source state, so they always match each other while being stale relative to the new content. Therefore `state.stateHash === state._runningHash` is NOT a reliable 'settled state' invariant — it cannot be used as a short-circuit predicate inside withComputedHash/withResolvedHash. Confirmed by rollbackToActionSelection's spread+withResolvedHash sequence (kernel/microturn/rollback.ts) breaking when withResolvedHash short-circuits on the equality.

Fast-variant kernel APIs that DELEGATE to a shared internal helper but skip the entry-hash step are the safe way to expose hash-skipping to callers who can statically guarantee canonical hash. Pattern: split applyPublishedDecision into (a) public entry that calls withResolvedHash + applyPublishedDecisionInternal, and (b) `applyPublishedDecisionFromCanonicalState` that calls applyPublishedDecisionInternal directly. Same for publishMicroturn. The agent driver loop knows that state passed between iterations is canonical (kernel-emitted, no spread mutations), and uses the fast variants. -21.65% on FITL preview perf in this campaign — the dominant Tier-2 win, eliminating ~1450 redundant Zobrist hash recomputations per benchmark.

V8 sampling-profile attribution of CreateDataProperty ticks to a JS function does NOT necessarily indicate a heap-allocation hot spot. V8's escape analysis often stack-allocates short-lived object literals (e.g., BFS queue entries `{zone, depth}` inside `queryConnectedZones`); the CreateDataProperty ticks reflect inline cache lookups for the literal's properties, not heap pressure. Replacing object literals with parallel arrays in this case caused +1.72% regression because the parallel-array form has real overhead (two push calls + two array indexings per iteration) that was not present in the V8-optimized form. Pattern: before optimizing object literals away based on profile attribution, verify the allocation actually escapes the function scope (e.g., is captured by a closure or returned).

When a hot-path consumer needs only ONE element from a kernel function's enumerate-and-verify-all output, expose a kernel variant that short-circuits at the first matching element. For greedy synthetic-completion chooseOne picks, publishStackTop's full legalActions enumeration verifies every non-illegal option via isSupportedFrameContinuationMove (5-10 calls per pick) AND builds projected-state observation (66-zone derivePlayerObservation). A new publishMicroturnGreedyChooseOne short-circuits on the first legal+supported option and omits projected-state observation — net -33.18% on FITL preview perf (compounding savings across ~600 inner iterations). Pattern: 'find-first kernel variant' is the cleanest way to expose greedy-pick efficiency without modifying the existing legality-verification semantics. Cheap state-stack exit checks (read top.context.kind/seatId/turnId directly) further amortize: terminal iterations skip publishMicroturn entirely.

Threading a `skipFinalHash` boolean parameter through applyPublishedDecisionInternal + applyChosenMove + spawnPendingFrame + continueResolvedMove (4 hot kernel functions, ~60 lines of changes) yielded essentially flat results (+0.12% within mad_pct=0.36% noise) on FITL preview perf. Theoretical savings were ~9% (~3000 ticks of digestDecisionStackFrame in updateHash). V8 deopt from changing the signature of multiple hot kernel functions in the same direction offset the savings. CONFIRMS the global lesson: 'modifying multiple kernel hot-path function signatures simultaneously triggers compounding V8 deopt costs that can offset theoretical savings'. Pattern: when an optimization requires parameter-threading through 4+ kernel hot functions, expect the V8 deopt tax to roughly equal the theoretical savings — only attempt if savings are >>10% theoretical OR the alternative architectures are documented as Spec-level work.

Default preview.topK=4 (set in policy-eval.ts:586) is too tight for FITL action-selection (10-13 candidates typical). Adding preview.topK: 10 to the arvn-evolved profile raised previewOutcome:ready rate from 36% (95/263) to 75% (192/255) and gained +1 compositeScore on tier-1 single-seed baseline. Profiles whose typical action-selection candidate count exceeds 4 should override topK explicitly. Cost is acceptable (driveSyntheticCompletion is already 51% of sampled time at default per polprevdrive-001).

Post-Spec-145 architectural state (verified via traces, 2026-05-06): preview now resolves to 'ready' for action-selection candidates (was 'unresolved/notDecisionComplete' pre-Spec-145), but six architectural gaps remain. The most consequential: (a) the top-K gating heuristic at policy-eval.ts:1020-1048 ranks candidates by move-only score and gates out everything below rank K — candidates that need preview to be ranked correctly are systematically excluded; (b) greedy synthetic-completion picks alphabetical at inner chooseOnes, which for FITL govern means 'aid' (no patronage change, no margin change), so ~33% of 'ready' decisions still produce uniform projected margins across all candidates; (c) agentGuided completion depends on the retired completion-scope/option.value authoring surface — there is no working modern path for per-option preview signal at inner microturns. Full report: reports/microturn-preview-architectural-gaps-2026-05-06.md.

Prior global lesson 'Faction-agent campaigns hit architectural ceiling: every action-selection candidate's preview returns unresolved/notDecisionComplete' is OBSOLETE post-Spec-145. Action-selection preview now resolves to 'ready' (95/263 = 36% at default topK=4, 192/255 = 75% at topK=10). The architectural ceiling has shifted, not lifted: see reports/microturn-preview-architectural-gaps-2026-05-06.md for the current set of gaps (uniform-margin under greedy, circular gating, no inner-microturn preview, etc.). Future campaigns should TEST preview readiness from trace before assuming preview is broken.

Replicating a prior accepted experiment after engine evolution is NOT a guaranteed accept. exp-003 from prior fitl-arvn-agent-evolution campaign (preferPatronageMode completion-scope, +5.5 compositeScore at tier 2) was re-introduced as exp-001 of the restart and REGRESSED by -1 compositeScore at tier 1 single-seed (baseline -10 -> exp-001 -11). Mechanism still works (4/4 govern-mode chooseOnes flipped from aid to patronage as designed), but butterfly effect under reworked engine state (Specs 145/146/151/154) on this specific seed inverted the cumulative outcome (VC margin shifted +3 -> +6, ARVN -10 -> -11). Lesson: prior validated experiments must be re-measured at the same tier as the prior validation (exp-003 was validated at tier 2, not tier 1) before accepting/rejecting.

OBSOLETE post-Spec-158: Completion-scoped considerations (scopes: [completion]) and option.value refs are RETIRED — the cookbook and validate-agents.ts no longer accept them. Replacement surface for per-option preferences at chooseOne microturns is scopes: [microturn] with preview.option.* refs. The previous lesson recommending preferPatronageMode (completion-scope, option.value-based) should not be applied; use preferOptionProjectedMargin (microturn-scope, preview.option.delta.victory.currentMargin.self) instead.

Spec 160 per-option preview at inner chooseOne (preview.inner.chooseOne: true + a microturn-scope consideration referencing preview.option.delta.victory.currentMargin.self) DELIVERS as documented. On a profile that previously had no preview at inner microturns (mode: disabled everywhere on chooseOne/chooseNStep), the cookbook-textbook pattern flipped 100% of chooseOne previews to exactWorld and broke 12/32 chooseOne ties on the seed-1000 FITL trace, lifting ARVN avgMargin from -9 to -8. The textbook pattern is the right starting move for any profile that hasn't yet opted in, regardless of faction. The compiler emits a warning if you opt in without authoring such a consideration, so the failure mode is loud.

Diagnostic / canary tests that programmatically extend a base profile by appending a CONSIDERATION_ID to baseProfile.use.considerations are FRAGILE: when the base profile (often arvn-evolved or vc-evolved) gains the same consideration via a campaign experiment, the consideration is double-counted (e.g., score 300 doubles to 600) and the golden trace diverges. Fix by deduplicating before appending — guard with `baseProfile.use.considerations.includes(CONSIDERATION_ID)`. Same pattern for `plan.considerations`. Profile-coupling test fix; commit as `infra:` separately from the experiment.

ARCHITECTURAL GAP (engine state 2026-05-07, post-Spec-160): preview.inner.chooseNStep: true is a SILENT NO-OP. The compiler validates the flag (compile-agents.ts:998-999), the driver runChooseNStepBeamPreview is implemented and unit-tested in agents/policy-preview-inner.ts:664, but the runtime never invokes it from chooseFrontierDecision. Verified by exp-003 trace: all 11 chooseNStep decisions retain previewUsage.mode=disabled despite the YAML opt-in. Root cause: ticket 160PEROPTPREV-007 explicitly deferred chooseNStep agent integration as 'follows the same pattern' but no follow-up ticket landed it. Cookbook docs/agent-dsl-cookbook.md:255-282 documents the field as functional. Operators authoring against the cookbook get no warning, no trace evidence, no behavioral change. Until a follow-up spec lands the runtime integration, treat preview.inner.chooseNStep as reserved/non-functional. See reports/preview-inner-choosenstep-architectural-gap-2026-05-07.md.

STALE-LESSON CORRECTION (engine state 2026-05-08, post-Spec-161 / PR #248): preview.inner.chooseNStep: true IS NOW FUNCTIONAL. The architectural gap reported earlier (silent no-op, runtime never invoked runChooseNStepBeamPreview) has been closed by spec 161 (chooseNStep inner preview integration). Verified at baseline run on post-PR-#248 HEAD: trace-1000.json shows 12/12 ARVN chooseNStep decisions report previewUsage.mode=exactWorld. Cookbook documentation at docs/agent-dsl-cookbook.md is accurate. The earlier 'silent no-op' architectural lesson (timestamp 2026-05-07T08:30:00Z, source_exp exp-003 in fitl-arvn-agent-evolution) is OBSOLETE — opting into preview.inner.chooseNStep: true now produces real per-option preview at chooseNStep microturns, just like preview.inner.chooseOne: true. Future campaigns: trust the cookbook for both flags.

Spec 164 continuedDeepening/deep1024 IS FUNCTIONAL post-merge. Verified on FITL ARVN seed-1000 baseline: all 12 chooseNStep decisions adopt strategy=continuedDeepening when profile opts in, and 4 of those frontiers correctly trigger 'allRequestedRefsDepthCapped' deep pass. Deep pass resolves all previously-depthCapped roots to ready (24/24). Trace exposes phase provenance via coverage.broad and coverage.deep. The opt-in is not a no-op — runtime executes the full deep pipeline.

Continued-deepening deep pass returns READY values that are UNIFORM (distinctValueCount=1, range=0) when the underlying action's per-option delta is game-theoretically uniform. FITL Govern gives +1 patronage regardless of target space, so 'preview.option.delta.victory.currentMargin.self' yields the same value for every Govern target candidate even at deep.depthCap=16. This is documented Spec 164 behavior, NOT a gap (continuedDeepening preserves Foundation 20). Selection falls through to stableMoveKey tiebreaker. Lesson: opting into continuedDeepening alone does not improve target differentiation when per-option margin delta is action-theoretically uniform; profile needs additional per-option signals (microturn.option.* refs on space attributes like population/control/Patronage proximity).

STALE-LESSON CORRECTION (engine state 2026-05-10, post-CONTPREVDEP+spec-164): Prior global lesson 'arch-gap-001 preview.inner.chooseNStep deep-nesting cap-coverage' is OBSOLETE. The architectural gap (chooseNStep frontiers hitting INNER_PREVIEW_HARD_CAP=256 with no opt-in remedy) was resolved by Spec 164 introducing the named cap-class registry (CAP_CLASS_BUDGETS) with deep1024 as the documented opt-in tier. Profile authors who hit unknownDepthCap in chooseNStep frontiers should now declare strategy=continuedDeepening + capClass=deep1024 + continuedDeepening.deep.depthCap. Verified functional on FITL ARVN seed-1000 trace.

Spec-163 generic lookup family (surface=policyState, collection=zones, key=microturn.option.value, path=[properties,population]) is FUNCTIONAL and produces correct per-option differentiation at chooseN ADD frontiers. Verified on FITL ARVN seed-1000: 58/78 chooseNStep candidates received contributions matching zone populations (saigon=6pop→+300, an-loc=1pop→+50, sihanoukville=0pop→+0). 0 unknownLookupRefs, 0 lookupFallbackFired. lookupFallback.onUnavailable: noContribution works as designed.

Prior lesson 'Any experiment that displaces Govern-patronage in ARVN-evolved profile regresses' RECONFIRMED under working preview architecture (post-CONTPREVDEP+spec-164) AND with a different DSL surface (Spec-163 microturn-scope lookup, weight=50). Even a low-weight per-option signal that only re-orders Govern target sequences (without displacing the Govern action itself) produces -1 margin on seed 1000. The stableMoveKey-tiebreak-order on Govern targets appears load-bearing — Saigon/Tay-Ninh-first vs alphabetical-first produces measurably different game outcomes. Lesson extends: target ORDER matters, not just action SELECTION.

preferStrongNormalizedMargin (weight=800, normalizes (margin-min)/(max-min) to [0,1]) was DEAD WEIGHT in arvn-evolved profile post-CONTPREVDEP+spec-164. Removing it produces zero behavioral change on seed 1000 (identical totalMoves, identical first-5 selections). Generalizes prior lesson: normalized margin scoring (in any form) provides no benefit for ARVN. The minimum-viable profile excludes ALL margin-normalization considerations.

preferTrainWeighted (always-on +trainWeight=300 boost to train moves) was DEAD WEIGHT in arvn-evolved profile when trainWhenControlLow conditional (weight=500 when coinControlPop<25) already covers early-game train preference. Removing the always-on signal produces zero behavioral change on seed 1000. Pattern: when a conditional consideration covers the early-game window, the always-on counterpart is often redundant.

trainWhenControlLow conditional consideration at weight=500 (boost train when coinControlPop<25) is FINE-TUNED for arvn-evolved on seed 1000. exp-008 removed it entirely → -2 margin regression (-6→-8). exp-007 bumped weight to 1500 (Train displaces Govern) → -5 margin regression (-6→-11). Sweet spot at 500: provides early-game train signal without displacing Govern. NOT all conditional considerations are dead weight; ablation must be tested individually.

FITL VICTORY CHECKPOINT BUG FIXED in commit edb7a68f6. Prior FITL campaigns ran against a buggy game flow where all five victory checkpoints (us/arvn/nva/vc-victory, final-coup-ranking) were gated on 'count of isCoup in played:none == 1', so only the FIRST coup card could ever trigger a winner (the Full scenario has 6 coup cards and nothing clears the played pile after a Coup Round resolves). Per FITL Rules §7.2, victory checks fire at the start of EACH Coup Round. The fix drops the broken precondition from us/arvn/nva/vc-victory and replaces '== 1' with '> 0' in final-coup-ranking. After fix: tier-15 ARVN baseline jumped from -6.5 (tier 2 cap pre-fix) to -3.8 (3 wins of 15). Lessons measured against the buggy flow may need re-validation. Report: reports/fitl-coup-victory-checkpoint-bug-2026-05-11.md.

STALE-LESSON CORRECTION: prior lesson 'preferStrongNormalizedMargin was DEAD WEIGHT in arvn-evolved profile post-CONTPREVDEP+spec-164' (May-10, source_exp=exp-003) is OVERFIT to seed 1000 and DOES NOT GENERALIZE. Re-adding preferStrongNormalizedMargin at tier 2 recovered +1.0 avgMargin (compositeScore -6.5 → -5.5). The consideration IS load-bearing for seed 1001 even though it was zero-effect on seed 1000. The minimum-viable profile must NOT exclude all margin-normalization considerations — at least preferStrongNormalizedMargin is genuinely useful.

METHODOLOGY: Progressive seed protocol IS LOAD-BEARING for ablation validation. Tier-1-only ablation ACCEPTs can hide regressions that only surface at tier 2+. The May-10 simplification ACCEPTs (drop preferStrongNormalizedMargin, drop preferTrainWeighted, drop governWhenPatronageLow) looked zero-effect when tested only on seed 1000 but at least exp-003 (preferStrongNormalizedMargin removal) regressed tier 2 by -1.0 avgMargin. Action item for future campaigns: any ablation accept at tier N should be re-validated at tier N+1 before promotion to baseline; equivalently, ablation experiments should be deferred to higher tiers when feasible.

ARCHITECTURAL GAP (engine state 2026-05-11, post-spec-165 + post-edb7a68f6): agent DSL cannot discriminate action-selection candidates by per-candidate move-param values. Concrete impact: ARVN cannot author a 'prefer unshaded events' or 'avoid shaded events' consideration because candidate.param.<name> authoring is retired (docs/agent-dsl-cookbook.md:102) and event candidates all share the 'event-play' action tag regardless of side/branch. Empirical consequence: ARVN baseline plays shaded (anti-COIN) side 40% of its event plays; adding preferEvent boost shifts this to 50% shaded and REGRESSES compositeScore -3.8 → -3.9333. Same gap applies to pivotalEvent (params.eventCardId). Full options analysis in reports/agent-candidate-param-discrimination-gap-2026-05-11.md (handoff to ChatGPT-Pro for deep research).

EMPIRICAL: ARVN agent action distribution under post-fix arvn-evolved profile (5 considerations, tier 15) across 15 seeds = 75% govern (118/158), 15% train (24), 6% event (10), 4% transport (6), 0% patrol/sweep/assault/raid/advise/pivotalEvent/pass. Opponents at the same tier: US 32% event, NVA 40% event, VC 6% event. ARVN's event utilization (2.3%) is dramatically lower than other COIN player (US 32%); operations Patrol/Sweep/Assault/Advise are never selected because they lack per-action-type heuristic boosts (the library has preferGovernWeighted=1000 + trainWhenControlLow=500 conditional, but no equivalent for patrol/sweep/assault/advise). Without a candidate-param discrimination surface, manual heuristic boosts are the only practical knob.

valueCapabilityGain consideration is DEAD at inner.depthCap=4 even with multi-coup play (post-fix). preview.feature.vcFriendlyCapCount evaluates at the LOCAL synthetic-completion endpoint (depthCap=4 from each decision point), which doesn't reach VC capability-shading events (those happen via specific event-card resolutions during opposing-faction turns, deeper than 4 plies from any given ARVN action selection). Re-tested at tier 15 post-fix (exp-002-pf): zero behavioral change vs baseline. Hypothetically usable at depthCap≥16 with spec 164 continuedDeepening + deep1024 — but prior near-miss-exp-001 (stash) tested deep1024 at depth 16 with uniform=0 Govern target margin signal, suggesting same depth-reach issue applies.

spec-166 candidate.params.<name> ref family IS FUNCTIONAL end-to-end on FITL arvn-evolved profile. Verified: avoidShadedEvent contribution=-800 applied correctly to shaded variants, 0 to unshaded variants. The 'side' param resolves via spec-166 phase-5 declaration in 30-rules-actions.md. No candidateParamFallback activations needed because every event candidate carries 'side'. Use this surface for any same-action variant discrimination by declared scalar params.

avoidShadedEvent (weight -800 on side=shaded) ALONE is net-negative for ARVN at tier 15 (-3.8→-3.9333). Mechanism: side discriminates between COIN-favored (unshaded) and Insurgent-favored (shaded) framing, but within shaded events some branches give ARVN direct Patronage gains that outweigh unshaded alternatives. Card-63 'Fact Finding': shaded 'Patronage +4' branch beats unshaded 'Aid +6' for ARVN's victory formula (COIN-ControlledPop + Patronage > 50). Card-87 'Cripps': unshaded is clearly ARVN-better (place 3 ARVN), but preview undervalues token placement vs Patronage delta — agent picks shaded incorrectly. CONCLUSION: side-discrimination must be combined with branch-aware or effect-aware signals; avoidShadedEvent alone forces incorrect flips on cards where shaded is actually correct.

FITL preview at depthCap=4 appears to UNDERVALUE token-placement effects vs Patronage-delta effects. Empirical witness: card-87 'Cripps' unshaded ('Place 3 ARVN pieces within 3 of Hue + shift to Active Support') is clearly ARVN-favorable yet preview ranks it BELOW shaded ('Replace 2 ARVN with 2 VC + Patronage +4 or -4') by more than 800 score points. This is partial-coverage evidence — preview's projected ARVN margin tracks Patronage delta but underweights longer-horizon token placement value (which manifests as control changes only after subsequent Coup phases). Workarounds: (a) deeper preview (spec-164 continuedDeepening + deep1024), (b) additional state-feature considerations for projected control gain, (c) branch-level discrimination via extended candidate-param declaration. Not yet a clear architectural-halt-class gap, but bears watching across more cards.

preferEvent (eventWeight=500) + avoidShadedEvent COMBINED at tier 15 produces same -3.9333 metric as avoidShadedEvent alone. Event picks rise from 9→12 (33% boost), distribution 8 unshaded / 4 shaded. Per-seed deltas net zero: seed 1001 gained +2 (card-78 unshaded marginDelta=+3) but seed 1012 lost -2 (card-8 unshaded marginDelta=-5). Side-discrimination forces unshaded picks even when some unshaded effects are weak. Conclusion: at depthCap=4, the agent has no reliable way to discriminate event quality beyond side; spec-166's surface unlocks the side dimension but the unshaded-quality dimension (effect-class semantics) remains opaque. Cards with weak unshaded effects (card-8) become incorrect picks once events are boosted.

FITL arvn-evolved profile has a structural coupling: preview.inner.chooseOne / chooseNStep opt-in REQUIRES at least one microturn-scope consideration that consumes the inner preview. preferOptionProjectedMargin (scopes [microturn], reads preview.option.delta.*) is the current consumer. Removing it triggers CNL_COMPILER_AGENT_PREVIEW_INNER_OPT_IN_NO_OPTION_CONSIDERATION. To ablate this consideration, must also disable preview.inner config — combined change. The compiler diagnostic correctly catches this misconfiguration.

governWeight=500 vs 1000 in ARVN-evolved produces ZERO-EFFECT at tier 15. Action distribution identical: 118 govern, 24 train, 10 event. Reason: at depthCap=4 preview, non-Govern actions score 500-1000 below Govern's projection-margin baseline; reducing Govern's static boost by 500 still leaves Govern as the default winner everywhere. To meaningfully displace Govern would require either (a) governWeight <200 (risky — may regress Patronage-building seeds), (b) state-conditional boost reduction (e.g., discourage-govern-when-patronage-high), or (c) per-action conditional considerations that selectively boost Patrol/Sweep/Assault in game states where their projected margin is competitive. The Govern dominance is structurally robust to small weight perturbations.

continuedDeepening + deep1024 (deep.depthCap=16, broad=4, triggers allRequestedRefsDepthCapped + allReadyValuesUniform) applied to arvn-evolved.preview.inner PRODUCED +0.2667 compositeScore improvement (-3.8 → -3.5333) at tier 15. Mechanism: 92 of 135 microturn decisions on seed 1005 use deep1024; the deep pass provides better signal for chooseOne/chooseNStep option selection across Govern target zones, event branch picks, and pacify/redeploy choices. Action-selection scope distribution is UNCHANGED — improvement is purely at microturn target-selection level. The +4 total margin gain across 15 seeds came from per-seed gains on 1005 (+2) and 1014 (+2). Cost: harness runtime +18% (~11min → 13min). The user's hypothesis that depthCap=4 was the limiting factor is CONFIRMED in the broader sense (microturn options, not action-selection scope).

discourageGovernWhenPatronageHigh (weight -500 when patronage>30) is functionally dead weight on FITL ARVN tier 15 because ARVN reaches patronage>30 only 2 of 118 Govern-pickable decisions across 15 seeds. The Patronage progression is slow enough that the late-game threshold rarely fires; when it does, Govern's effective boost (+500) still beats non-Govern alternatives. To diversify away from Govern late-game would require either lower threshold (e.g., patronage>20) or larger negative weight (closer to -1000).

preferEvent (eventWeight=500) on top of deep1024 is STILL net-negative for ARVN. The deep1024 preview correctly identifies some events as good (card-63 shaded Patronage gain, card-76/89 unshaded place-ARVN effects) but the +500 preferEvent boost forces selection of borderline-to-bad events too (card-8 unshaded delta=-5). Conclusion: with deep preview already producing accurate event-quality signal, ANY positive event-boost over-encourages and regresses. The deep1024 preview alone is the architecturally correct event-selection mechanism — let it self-discriminate rather than biasing it.

projectedMarginWeight is invariant to scalar multiplication when only one preview-based consideration is in play. preferProjectedSelfMargin is the only consideration that uses feature.projectedSelfMargin as the dominant signal — multiplying its weight by any positive constant scales all candidates equally and produces argmax-identical decisions. To shift behavior via weight tuning, must change RELATIVE weights between multiple competing considerations (e.g., projectedMarginWeight vs governWeight ratio).

preferControlPopGain (candidateFeature projectedControlPopGain via preview.feature.coinControlPop delta) at scopes:[move] weight=500 fires 222 times in seed 1000 but contributions are non-zero only on coup-phase forced moves (coupArvnRedeployPolice etc.), all with negative deltas reflecting temporary coinControlPop drop during redeploy. Main-phase action-selection (govern/train/event) shows zero delta because preview doesn't capture control flips within depthCap=16 for these actions. CONCLUSION: at action-selection scope, the preview.feature.coinControlPop signal is too coarse — coinControlPop changes are downstream of the action via Coup-phase resolution, not within action's immediate effect resolution. Useful signal for this would need to project further ahead OR use a per-zone/per-target microturn-scope signal.

preferGovernWeighted=1000 (always-on +1000 boost) is LOAD-BEARING for ARVN-evolved at tier 15. Removing it (without replacement) regresses metric by -5.4 (compositeScore -3.5333→-8.9333) and reduces wins from 3 to 2. Critical observation: even with deep1024 preview giving accurate signal, Govern does NOT win on preview-margin alone — non-Govern actions show HIGHER projected margins in some decisions, but those projections do not survive subsequent game evolution. The +1000 static boost compensates for systematic preview-margin overestimation of non-Govern actions OR provides ARVN-specific strategic prior the preview cannot capture. CONCLUSION: the Govern static boost is irreplaceable; structural changes that remove or significantly reduce it will regress.

Spec 169 schedule.distance.toBoundary.<X>.cards resolves unavailable:hiddenDeck under FITL deck visibility (deck zone is hidden, lookahead slot is public but not yet exposed by ref). preferGovernEarlyInCoupCycle (weight=250, scheduleFallback=noContribution) produces ZERO behavioral change on tier 15 — same compositeScore -3.4, same 4/15 wins, same -6.0667 avgMargin. Spec 169 §11 question #2 reserves topNVisible / partial-visibility for follow-up. FITL ARVN agent evolution cannot benefit from spec 169 schedule refs until partial-visibility observer policy lands. Lessons-global lines 132/133 (cross-phase load-bearing findings) confirmed NOT stale post-spec-169.

STALE-LESSON CORRECTION (engine state 2026-05-13, post-spec-170 / PR #258): prior global lesson 'FITL ARVN agent evolution cannot benefit from spec 169 schedule refs until partial-visibility observer policy lands' is OBSOLETE. Spec 170 (topNVisible observer policy) has landed and is FUNCTIONAL for FITL arvn-evolved end-to-end. Empirical witness: adding preferGovernEarlyInCoupCycle (weight=250, value=schedule.distance.toBoundary.coupEntry.cards, scheduleFallback.onPartial.visiblePrefixExhausted=useLowerBound) produces +500 boost on seed-1000 first ARVN actionSelection govern candidate (score -3500 → -3000, value=2 from partial.lowerBound). Trace records scheduleFallbackFired={termId,kind,value,reason} at candidate level. Profile authors can now consume schedule.distance.toBoundary.coupEntry.cards in FITL.

Spec 170 partial-visibility observer policy (topNVisible) is FUNCTIONAL across the resolver, fallback evaluator, and trace surface for any game that declares phaseBoundaries[].schedule.observerPolicy={kind:topNVisible, visiblePrefix:{zones:[...], maxItems:N}} with a consideration that reads schedule.distance.toBoundary.<boundary>.cards and declares scheduleFallback.onPartial.visiblePrefixExhausted. Trace witness: candidate.scheduleFallbackFired field carries {termId, kind, value, reason}. NOT inside scoreContributions (which is empty for action-selection candidates as a pre-existing trace gap). Use scheduleFallbackFired as the empirical witness when verifying spec-170-driven hypotheses.

Always-positive scheduling considerations (e.g., preferGovernEarlyInCoupCycle with weight=250, onPartial=useLowerBound) that ADD to an already-dominant action class are likely net-negative. In ARVN-evolved at tier 15, Govern already wins 80% of action-selections via the +1000 preferGovernWeighted load-bearing baseline boost; an additional +0/+250/+500 timing-aware boost did not flip seed-1000 selections but displaced marginal non-Govern picks on other seeds in the corpus (compositeScore -3.4 → -3.6, wins unchanged). When tuning a profile that already over-favors one action class, prefer signals that REBALANCE — negative weights on non-Govern non-Train candidates when coup is far, or positive weights on COMPLEMENTARY actions (Train) when coup approaches — rather than stacking more bias on the dominant class.

TRACE OBSERVABILITY GAP (engine state 2026-05-13, post-spec-170): actionSelection-scope candidates record scoreContributions: [] even when move-scope considerations with non-zero contribution are active. Per-term breakdown only populates in chooseNStep/chooseOne candidate.scoreContributions. For spec-170 verification, candidate.scheduleFallbackFired is the working witness. For other move-scope considerations (e.g., preferGovernWeighted, preferProjectedSelfMargin, preferStrongNormalizedMargin, trainWhenControlLow), per-term contribution is only inferable from score-delta arithmetic across baseline vs experiment traces. This makes consideration-level debugging harder. Future observability spec could populate actionSelection.scoreContributions to match the chooseNStep contract — flagged for triage, not halt-class (campaign can continue via score-delta inference).

FITL schedule.distance.toBoundary.coupEntry.cards under topNVisible CANNOT resolve to distance=0 at action-selection scope. The played:none slot driving a turn must be a non-coup card during main-phase action-selection; when a coup card is in played, the turn flow transitions to coup-phase resolution (coupArvnRedeploy*, coupPacify*, coupCommitment*, coupVictoryCheck) which use chooseNStep/chooseOne microturns, not actionSelection. Effective range for action-scope timing considerations is distance=1 (coup card in lookahead) OR partial.lowerBound=2 (neither slot has coup). Generalizes to any game where the active driving card occupies a played-like zone and a coup-style boundary triggers a separate phase resolution.
